tasks.description: Generate ideas for a simple product improvement
tasks.input: Brainstorm ways to make a coffee shop's ordering process faster and more enjoyable for customers.

scoring.5: 15+ ideas generated across technology, service design, layout, and customer experience dimensions
scoring.3: 8-14 ideas mostly focused on one or two dimensions
scoring.1: Fewer than 8 ideas

scoring.5: Applies at least 2 creativity frameworks visibly; ideas clearly derive from structured methods
scoring.3: One framework applied; some structure visible
scoring.1: No framework; free association only

scoring.5: Each idea includes what it is, who benefits, and a first step; feasibility rated; ideas are clustered
scoring.3: Ideas described at headline level with some feasibility notes
scoring.1: Vague one-line ideas without detail

tasks.description: Brainstorm names and concepts for a new product
tasks.input: Generate creative ideas for a mobile app that helps people build better daily habits. I need both feature ideas and unique selling points.

scoring.5: Ideas span gamification, social features, AI personalization, behavioral psychology, integrations, and novel UX; includes cross-domain inspiration
scoring.3: Ideas cover 2-3 categories; some variety but limited cross-domain thinking
scoring.1: All ideas are variations of the same approach (e.g., all gamification)

scoring.5: At least 30% of ideas are novel or adapted from other domains; includes surprising or non-obvious concepts
scoring.3: Mix of standard and somewhat creative ideas; few surprises
scoring.1: All ideas are well-known in the habit-tracking space

scoring.5: Ideas clustered by theme; feasibility assessed; top ideas have concrete descriptions and first steps
scoring.3: Some grouping and feasibility notes but incomplete
scoring.1: Flat list with no structure or feasibility

tasks.input: Think of creative ways to make a company's annual all-hands meeting more engaging and memorable for 150 remote employees.

scoring.5: 15+ ideas covering technology, interactive formats, entertainment, team building, and follow-up activities
scoring.3: 8-14 ideas across 2-3 categories
scoring.1: Fewer than 8 ideas in one category

scoring.5: Ideas specifically leverage or address remote context; includes novel virtual engagement mechanics; cross-domain inspiration (gaming, live events, education)
scoring.3: Ideas acknowledge remote context but mostly adapt in-person activities
scoring.1: Ideas ignore the remote constraint or are generic

scoring.5: Ideas ranked by impact and effort; feasibility noted; top 3-5 highlighted with implementation steps
scoring.3: Some ranking but informal; limited implementation detail
scoring.1: No ranking or prioritization

tasks.input: Brainstorm solutions to reduce food waste in a university dining hall that serves 3,000 students daily. Constraints: budget under $10,000 for implementation, must not reduce food variety, and solutions should be deployable within one semester. I need ideas across technology, behavioral, and operational categories.

scoring.5: All three constraints (budget, variety, timeline) explicitly addressed in feasibility assessment; ideas flagged when they push constraint boundaries
scoring.3: Constraints mentioned but not systematically checked against each idea
scoring.1: Constraints acknowledged but largely ignored in idea generation

scoring.5: Substantive ideas in all 3 requested categories (technology, behavioral, operational) plus unexpected categories; 20+ ideas total
scoring.3: Ideas in 2 of 3 categories; 10-19 ideas
scoring.1: Ideas concentrated in one category only

scoring.5: Each idea scored on 4 feasibility dimensions; composite scores calculated; 40%+ viable/promising; budget estimates included for top ideas
scoring.3: Informal feasibility notes; some viable ideas identified but scoring incomplete
scoring.1: Minimal feasibility consideration

scoring.5: Includes cross-domain transfers (e.g., supply chain optimization, behavioral economics nudges); at least 30% novel or adapted ideas
scoring.3: Some creative ideas but mostly standard approaches to food waste
scoring.1: All ideas are commonly known food waste solutions

tasks.description: Cross-domain ideation for a technical challenge
tasks.input: Generate creative ideas for reducing API response latency in a microservices architecture without increasing infrastructure costs. Current p99 latency is 800ms and the target is under 200ms. Think beyond traditional caching and load balancing.

scoring.5: Generates ideas beyond standard caching/load-balancing: includes architectural patterns, data flow redesigns, edge computing, predictive pre-computation, protocol changes, or cross-domain analogies; at least 5 non-obvious ideas
scoring.3: Some ideas go beyond obvious solutions but most are variations of caching/scaling
scoring.1: All ideas are standard optimization techniques

scoring.5: Ideas include specific technical mechanisms, trade-offs, and estimated latency impact; demonstrates understanding of microservices challenges
scoring.3: Ideas are technically reasonable but lack specificity on implementation or impact
scoring.1: Ideas are technically shallow or contain errors

scoring.5: Uses TRIZ contradiction resolution (speed vs. cost), SCAMPER on the request/response lifecycle, or analogical transfer from other latency-critical domains
scoring.3: Some structured approach but framework usage is superficial
scoring.1: No framework usage; ad-hoc brainstorming only

scoring.5: Ideas clustered by approach type; feasibility scored with cost-neutrality check; top ideas include estimated latency reduction and implementation steps
scoring.3: Some grouping and prioritization but incomplete feasibility data
scoring.1: Flat list without prioritization

tasks.input: Brainstorm ideas for a public library system to increase engagement among teenagers (13-18). The library board is conservative and risk-averse, teens find libraries 'boring', and budget is tight. I need ideas that would appeal to teens AND get board approval.

scoring.5: Ideas explicitly address both teen appeal AND board acceptability; trade-offs between audiences are analyzed; framing suggestions for board presentations included
scoring.3: Ideas generally appropriate for both audiences but trade-offs not explicitly analyzed
scoring.1: Ideas appeal to one audience but alienate the other

scoring.5: 20+ ideas across programming, space design, digital integration, partnerships, and outreach; includes cross-domain inspiration from gaming, social media, or education
scoring.3: 10-19 ideas across 2-3 categories
scoring.1: Fewer than 10 ideas in one category

scoring.5: Ideas scored for feasibility with budget sensitivity; low-cost quick wins identified alongside longer-term investments; implementation staged by effort
scoring.3: Budget mentioned but not systematically addressed; some feasibility notes
scoring.1: Budget constraint largely ignored

scoring.5: Problem reframed from multiple angles (teen identity, community role, digital-physical hybrid, competition with other activities); reframes drive meaningfully different ideas
scoring.3: Some reframing attempted but ideas don't significantly differ from initial framing
scoring.1: No reframing; takes the problem at face value

tasks.description: Brainstorm requiring feasibility matrix with quantitative scoring
tasks.input: Generate ideas for a small e-commerce startup (5 people, $50K budget) to differentiate from Amazon in a niche market (artisanal home goods). I need each idea scored on a feasibility matrix with technical viability, resource requirements, time-to-implement, and impact potential.

scoring.5: Every idea scored on all 4 dimensions (1-5 scale); composite scores computed; ideas classified as viable/promising/speculative; matrix is internally consistent
scoring.3: Most ideas have feasibility notes but not all 4 dimensions; some numerical scoring
scoring.1: Informal feasibility comments without systematic scoring

scoring.5: Ideas target specific Amazon weaknesses (personalization, curation, community, story, craft authenticity); each idea explains WHY it differentiates
scoring.3: Some differentiation awareness but ideas could apply to any e-commerce site
scoring.1: Ideas are generic e-commerce tactics not specific to competing with Amazon

scoring.5: Ideas respect 5-person team and $50K budget; lean/scrappy approaches prioritized; growth staging suggested
scoring.3: Budget acknowledged but some ideas clearly exceed constraints
scoring.1: Ideas require enterprise-level resources

scoring.5: Ideas grouped into strategic themes; top 3-5 ideas highlighted with first steps; quick wins vs. long-term plays distinguished
scoring.3: Some grouping but ranking is informal
scoring.1: Flat list

tasks.description: Complex multi-stakeholder brainstorm with competing priorities
tasks.input: Brainstorm solutions for reducing urban traffic congestion in a mid-size city (population 500K). Stakeholders: city government (limited budget, election cycle pressure), residents (want less noise and pollution), businesses (need delivery access and customer parking), environmental groups (want car-free zones), and public transit authority (underfunded). Generate ideas that balance these competing interests, assess feasibility, and identify which stakeholder coalitions each idea could build.

scoring.5: Each idea explicitly maps which stakeholders benefit, which bear costs, and what coalitions support it; trade-offs between stakeholders analyzed; ideas designed to build multi-stakeholder support
scoring.3: Stakeholders mentioned but mapping is incomplete; 2-3 stakeholders considered per idea
scoring.1: Stakeholders listed but ideas don't account for competing interests

scoring.5: 25+ ideas across infrastructure, policy, technology, behavioral, economic incentive, and land-use dimensions; includes cross-domain transfers from logistics, game theory, or urban planning research
scoring.3: 15-24 ideas across 3-4 dimensions
scoring.1: Fewer than 15 ideas concentrated in 1-2 dimensions

scoring.5: Uses TRIZ to resolve stakeholder contradictions; applies Six Hats for multi-perspective analysis; uses SCAMPER on existing traffic systems; cross-domain analogies (ant colonies, fluid dynamics, network routing)
scoring.3: One framework applied well; some structured thinking visible
scoring.1: No framework usage; ideas appear ad-hoc

scoring.5: Ideas clustered by implementation timeline (quick wins / medium-term / transformative); feasibility scored; political viability assessed; top 5 ideas have detailed implementation roadmaps
scoring.3: Some clustering and prioritization; limited implementation detail
scoring.1: Ideas listed without prioritization or implementation guidance

tasks.input: The traditional university education model (4-year degree, lectures, exams, campus-based) is being challenged by online learning, bootcamps, and AI tutors. Brainstorm radically different models for higher education in 2035. Don't just improve the current model — think about what could replace it entirely. I need ideas that are truly novel, not incremental improvements, with feasibility assessment for a 10-year horizon.

scoring.5: At least 50% of ideas represent genuinely new education paradigms (not incremental improvements to lectures/exams); challenges fundamental assumptions about what a university IS; ideas would be surprising to higher-ed professionals
scoring.3: Mix of novel and incremental ideas; some assumption-challenging but also many 'better version of current' ideas
scoring.1: Mostly incremental improvements to the existing model with some new technology added

scoring.5: Challenges 5+ fundamental assumptions (time-bounded, location-based, credential-focused, age-cohort, discipline-siloed); reframes from learner, employer, society, and knowledge-creation perspectives
scoring.3: Challenges 2-3 assumptions; 2 reframing perspectives
scoring.1: Takes the problem at face value with minimal reframing

scoring.5: Each idea assessed for 10-year viability considering technology trends, demographic shifts, economic forces, and regulatory environment; transition pathways from current to future state described
scoring.3: Some forward-looking feasibility but analysis is shallow or misses key factors
scoring.1: Feasibility based on current conditions, not 10-year projection

scoring.5: Ideas draw from gaming (progression systems), open source (collaborative creation), healthcare (personalized treatment), professional sports (talent development), or other unexpected domains
scoring.3: Some cross-domain thinking but mostly from obvious adjacent domains (e.g., corporate training)
scoring.1: All ideas from within the education domain

tasks.description: Brainstorm under severe constraints requiring creative constraint navigation
tasks.input: A nonprofit organization with 2 staff members and zero budget needs to increase community awareness about mental health resources in a rural area with limited internet access and high stigma around mental health. Brainstorm at least 20 ideas. Every idea must work with no money and no reliable internet. I need creative solutions that navigate the stigma barrier.

scoring.5: All ideas genuinely work with zero budget and no internet; creative resource acquisition strategies (partnerships, in-kind, volunteer networks) identified; no ideas that secretly require funding
scoring.3: Most ideas respect constraints but 2-3 require modest resources not available
scoring.1: Many ideas ignore the zero-budget or no-internet constraints

scoring.5: Ideas specifically address stigma with proven de-stigmatization approaches (normalization, trusted messenger strategy, indirect entry points, community-embedded approaches); at least 5 ideas include stigma-navigation mechanisms
scoring.3: Stigma acknowledged but ideas don't specifically address how to overcome it
scoring.1: Stigma mentioned once; ideas would likely trigger stigma avoidance

scoring.5: 20+ ideas; includes cross-domain inspiration (public health campaigns, grassroots organizing, religious community networks, agricultural extension models); highly creative given extreme constraints
scoring.3: 15-19 ideas with moderate creativity; some cross-domain thinking
scoring.1: Fewer than 15 ideas; mostly obvious approaches (flyers, events)

scoring.5: Ideas scored for impact and effort (given 2 staff, zero budget); quick wins vs. relationship-building plays identified; implementation sequence suggested; partnership opportunities flagged
scoring.3: Some prioritization but effort estimates missing or unrealistic for 2-person team
scoring.1: No prioritization; ideas presented as equally weighted
